hero.title: Paolo Lattanzi
hero.subtitle: Philosophy of Time · Analytic Metaphysics · Social Theory
hero.description: PhD student in Philosophy at Universität Siegen with a strong foundation in metaphysics and social theory, alongside over a decade of international experience in digital marketing and performance analytics. Passionate about research and teaching, with proven success as a university lecturer.

about.heading:
Bridging Philosophy
and the World
paragraphs: I am a PhD student in Philosophy at Universität Siegen, Germany, with a strong foundation in analytic metaphysics and social theory. My research focuses on Philosophy of Time, Ontology, the Foundations of Physics, and Analytical Social Theory.

Alongside my doctoral work, I serve as an Honorary Professor at IU Internationale Hochschule in Hamburg, where I teach Web Analytics and Search Engine Marketing — bridging academic inquiry with real-world digital innovation.

Prior to returning to academia full-time, I built over a decade of experience leading performance marketing teams across Hamburg, working with clients and organisations such as Essity, DAAD, and Allianz.

factCards.heading: Research Focus
factCards.body: Philosophy of Time, Ontology, Analytic Metaphysics, Foundations of Physics

research.pageHeroSubtitle: My work spans analytical philosophy of time, ontology, analytic metaphysics, foundations of physics, and analytical social theory.
research.homeSectionSubtitle: My work spans five interconnected fields within analytic philosophy and social theory.

areas.title: Philosophy of Time
areas.description: Investigating the metaphysical dimensions of temporal experience — passage, the present moment, McTaggart's A- and B-series, and the deep relationship between time, change, and causation.

areas.title: Analytic Metaphysics
areas.description: Engaging with core problems in analytic ontology — persistence through time, identity, modality, and the structure of reality as approached through the tools of analytic philosophy.

areas.title: Analytical Social Theory
areas.description: Applying analytic philosophical tools to questions of society, power, and political economy — including contributions to Marxist systems theory and international relations.

areas.title: Foundations of Physics
areas.description: Exploring the philosophical implications of fundamental physics — in particular, what special relativity reveals about the nature of time, space, and causation. The Twin Paradox and its implications for temporal passage are a central concern.

areas.title: Ontology
areas.description: Investigating the fundamental structure of reality through the methods of analytic ontology — questions of existence, identity, parthood, and the categories of being as they relate to time.

phdProject.heading: PhD Research at Universität Siegen
paragraphs: My doctoral research, conducted at Universität Siegen since 2023, sits at the intersection of Philosophy of Time, Analytical Metaphysics, and Ontology. The project develops a systematic account of temporal passage and its relationship to physical theory — in particular, to what special relativity tells us about time.

One strand of the research examines the Rate Argument — the question of whether time can coherently be said to "pass" at a rate — and its implications for A-theories of time. I presented early findings at the DGPhil 2024 conference under the title "The Rate Argument and the Twin Paradox."

A second strand investigates the relationship between tense and modality: how the grammatical marking of past, present, and future connects to claims about what is possible and what is necessary. I presented this work at SOPhiA 2023.

blog.pageHeroSubtitle: Essays, reflections, and short pieces on philosophy, time, politics, and their intersections.
blog.homeSectionSubtitle: Essays, reflections, and commentary on philosophy, politics, and time.

posts.title: The Rate Argument and the Twin Paradox
posts.excerpt: Can time genuinely pass at a rate — and what does special relativity's famous twin paradox reveal about that rate? I examine what this tells us about A-theories of temporal passage and whether the Rate Argument can survive relativistic physics.

posts.title: Tense and Modality
posts.excerpt: What is the relationship between tense — the grammatical marking of past, present, and future — and modal claims about what is possible and what is necessary? I explore tense logic and its connections to modal logic.

posts.date: On the paper published in Marxism & Sciences, 2026
posts.title: Towards a Marxist Systems Theory of International Relations
posts.excerpt: Standard IR theories share a common blind spot: they treat the international system as a set of interacting states rather than as a structured totality shaped by capitalist dynamics. Drawing on systems theory and Marxist political economy, I propose a framework centred on the concept of "uneven ties."

posts.date: On the chapter in Jahrbuch für Marxistische Gesellschaftstheorie, 2025
posts.title: Imperialismus heute und der Ukraine-Krieg
posts.excerpt: The war in Ukraine is not merely a conflict between two states — it is a node in the broader structure of inter-imperialist rivalry. From a historical-materialist standpoint, I reconstruct the economic and geopolitical forces that produced the conflict.

posts.title: McTaggart's A-Series: Still the Best Game in Town?
posts.excerpt: McTaggart's 1908 argument that time is unreal, and the A/B distinction that it rests on, remain central to the metaphysics of time. I examine why A-theories continue to attract defenders despite the challenges posed by physics and the rate argument.

posts.title: What Relativity Tells Us — and Doesn't Tell Us — About Time
posts.excerpt: Special relativity is often cited as decisive evidence against A-theories of time. If simultaneity is relative, there can be no objective "now." But does relativity really settle the metaphysical question, or does it merely shift its terms?

contact.pageHeroSubtitle: I welcome correspondence from students, researchers, and anyone with a genuine interest in the topics I work on.
contact.introText: Feel free to reach out about my research, collaboration opportunities, speaking invitations, or teaching enquiries. I try to respond to all messages within a week.
